name: Exterior window bands
description: Generous, regularly-spaced windows along every exterior wall of every habitable room, so that Alexander's light-on-two-sides principle is satisfied by construction. Works for medieval cottages with shuttered casements, modern minimalist clerestory bands, Japanese shoji-style paper panels, mediterranean arched openings, and Renaissance multi-pane fenestration — the pattern is the same (regular spacing, ≥2 walls per room), only the materials change per style. Applies to residential, tavern, temple, monument, and shop buildings of any size. The fitting reads each room's AABB, identifies which faces touch the building exterior, and places a band of windows on each — guaranteeing that every habitable room receives daylight from at least two cardinal directions whenever its footprint allows it.
placement_rules: For each habitable room, identify every wall face that lies on the building exterior.
Place 2-4 windows per exterior wall, spaced evenly along its length.
Each window is 1-2 cells wide and 2-3 cells tall, sill 1-2 cells above the floor.
If a room touches the exterior on ≥2 perpendicular axes, place windows on BOTH — this is the light-on-two-sides invariant.
Windows DO NOT pierce shared interior walls (the room's neighbor would see daylight from indoors).
Material choice deferred to style_variants in palette: glass_pane (generic), white_stained_glass_pane (renaissance), spruce_planks_with_glass (japanese).
